title: Project initiation
definition: Initiation is the phase where a project is formally authorized and its high-level purpose, boundaries, and leadership are established.
key_points: Formally authorizes the project or phase
Appoints/empowers the project manager (PM) to begin planning
Creates alignment on why the project exists
why_it_matters: Many exam questions test whether you understand what can and cannot be done before authorization—initiation prevents wasted effort and misaligned work.
example: An executive approves launching a new customer portal and assigns a PM, funding, and success criteria before detailed planning begins.
exam_trap: Starting detailed scope, schedule, or procurement planning before the project is authorized.

title: Business case
definition: A business case justifies the project by describing the business need, expected benefits, costs, risks, and alternatives.
key_points: Explains why the project should exist
why_it_matters: PMP questions often ask what document justifies a project and where the charter’s rationale comes from.
example: A hospital estimates reduced readmissions by implementing a new care coordination system; compares build vs buy.
exam_trap: Confusing the business case with the project charter; the charter authorizes, the business case justifies.

title: Benefits management plan
definition: A benefits management plan defines intended benefits, ownership, measurement approach, and timing—often beyond the project’s end date.
key_points: Benefits can be realized after delivery
why_it_matters: PMP scenarios test whether you connect projects to benefits realization, not just deliverables.
example: After deploying a new billing system, finance owns a 12‑month target to reduce claim denials by 15%.
exam_trap: Assuming benefits are realized at project close; deliverables ≠ benefits.

title: Project charter
definition: The project charter formally authorizes the project and gives the PM authority to apply organizational resources.
key_points: High-level scope, objectives, and success criteria
Names the PM and defines authority level
why_it_matters: Many PMP questions hinge on: if you don’t have a charter, you’re not authorized to proceed.
example: The sponsor signs the charter stating objective, budget cap, major milestones, and PM authority to staff a team.
exam_trap: Treating the charter like a detailed project management plan; it’s high-level and created early.

title: Charter contents (typical)
definition: A charter typically includes purpose, measurable objectives, high-level requirements, assumptions, constraints, risks, summary schedule and budget, and stakeholder list.
why_it_matters: Exam items often ask what belongs in the charter vs detailed plans/baselines.
example: Charter lists budget ≤ $500k, go-live by Q4, and key requirement: HIPAA compliance.
exam_trap: Putting WBS, detailed schedule network, or full risk register in the charter.

title: High-level requirements
definition: High-level requirements describe what the solution must do, broadly enough to guide planning without forcing design choices too early.
key_points: Express business needs at a high level
why_it_matters: PMP tests distinguishing high-level requirements (charter) from detailed requirements (requirements documentation).
example: “System must support multi-factor authentication for all staff users.”
exam_trap: Jumping straight to design specifications during initiation.

title: Assumptions
definition: Assumptions are factors considered true for planning purposes, which may later prove false and create risk.
why_it_matters: Many scenarios test what to do when an assumption changes: document, analyze impact, and manage.
example: Assuming a vendor API will be available by May; if delayed, schedule risk increases.
exam_trap: Treating assumptions as guaranteed facts and ignoring their risk impact.

title: Constraints
definition: Constraints are limiting factors that restrict options, such as fixed deadlines, budgets, resources, regulatory rules, or mandated tools.
key_points: Often set by sponsor or environment
why_it_matters: Constraints show up in many questions about feasibility and trade-offs.
example: A regulatory deadline forces go-live before year-end; overtime budget is capped.
exam_trap: Calling everything a constraint; distinguish constraints from assumptions and preferences.

title: Project selection methods (overview)
definition: Selection methods help organizations choose which projects to authorize based on strategy, value, risk, and constraints.
why_it_matters: PMP often tests NPV/IRR/payback plus the idea that strategy/compliance can override pure ROI.
example: A portfolio team ranks proposals using NPV and risk score to decide which to fund.
exam_trap: Assuming the PM chooses projects; selection is usually portfolio/governance.

title: Net present value (NPV)
definition: NPV is the present value of expected inflows minus outflows; positive NPV indicates value creation given the discount rate.
key_points: Accounts for time value of money
Higher NPV generally preferred (all else equal)
why_it_matters: NPV is a frequent exam calculation/interpretation topic.
example: Discounted benefits $1.2M and costs $900k → NPV = $300k.
exam_trap: Comparing cash flows without discounting or choosing lower NPV because it’s 'cheaper.'

title: Internal rate of return (IRR)
definition: IRR is the discount rate at which NPV equals zero; IRR above the hurdle rate is generally acceptable.
key_points: Compared to required rate of return
Less useful for irregular cash flows
why_it_matters: IRR is a common selection model topic.
example: Hurdle rate 8%, IRR 12% → generally attractive.
exam_trap: Assuming higher IRR always wins without considering risk/strategy.

title: Payback period
definition: Payback period is the time required for cumulative benefits to repay the initial investment.
key_points: Does not discount unless explicitly stated
why_it_matters: PMP tests method limitations and interpretation.
example: A $200k project returning $50k/month has payback ~4 months (ignoring discounting).
exam_trap: Using payback alone to compare long-term value projects.

title: Scoring models
definition: Scoring models rank projects against weighted criteria such as strategic alignment, risk, compliance, and ROI.
why_it_matters: Helps answer questions where projects have different benefits (financial vs compliance vs strategic).
example: Compliance weight 40%, revenue 30%, risk 30%; projects are scored and ranked.
exam_trap: Thinking scoring replaces governance judgment; it supports it.

title: Sponsor
definition: The sponsor provides resources, champions the project, and authorizes the charter.
why_it_matters: PMP tests roles/responsibilities and escalation paths.
example: A VP approves budget and clears roadblocks with other departments.
exam_trap: Assuming sponsor manages day-to-day work; that’s the PM’s role.

title: PM authority
definition: PM authority is the power granted to the PM to make decisions and apply resources, shaped by the charter and organizational structure.
why_it_matters: Scenarios often hinge on whether you can assign resources directly or must negotiate.
example: In a functional org, the PM negotiates with functional managers for staff time.
exam_trap: Assuming PM always has direct authority over resources.

title: Functional organization
definition: A functional organization groups staff by specialty; functional managers control resources and PM authority is typically low.
why_it_matters: Org structure affects how you obtain resources and resolve conflicts.
example: IT, Finance, and HR allocate part-time resources; approvals run through functional managers.
exam_trap: Expecting dedicated team members without negotiation.

title: Matrix organization (weak/balanced/strong)
definition: Matrix organizations share power between functional managers and PMs; PM authority varies from weak to strong.
why_it_matters: PMP tests structure identification based on authority and resource control.
example: Strong matrix gives PM budget control while functional managers manage careers.
exam_trap: Assuming 'matrix' always means high PM authority.

title: Projectized organization
definition: In a projectized organization, PMs have high authority and teams are often dedicated full time to projects.
why_it_matters: Provides contrast with functional/matrix for resource and authority questions.
example: A consulting firm assigns dedicated teams; PM controls budget and priorities.
exam_trap: Assuming projectized eliminates all resource constraints.

title: Stakeholder register
definition: A stakeholder register lists stakeholders and key details such as role, interest, influence, and engagement expectations.
why_it_matters: Often the correct answer when asked what document to update after discovering a stakeholder.
example: Register captures compliance officer’s influence and required review checkpoints.
exam_trap: Confusing the stakeholder register with the communications plan.

title: Power/interest grid
definition: A power/interest grid classifies stakeholders based on authority (power) and concern (interest) to guide engagement strategy.
why_it_matters: PMP tests choosing engagement strategies based on stakeholder classification.
example: A CFO (high power, low interest) wants summary updates rather than daily details.
exam_trap: Using a one-size-fits-all communication approach.

title: Enterprise environmental factors (EEFs)
definition: EEFs are internal/external conditions that influence or constrain the project, such as regulations, culture, marketplace, and infrastructure.
why_it_matters: PMP tests distinguishing EEFs from OPAs and recognizing constraints from the environment.
example: A new privacy law imposes consent and retention requirements.
exam_trap: Calling internal templates an EEF; templates are OPAs.

title: Organizational process assets (OPAs)
definition: OPAs are internal resources such as templates, policies, lessons learned, and historical information used to guide project work.
why_it_matters: PMP often asks whether something is an OPA or EEF.
example: Using a charter template and historical schedule data from similar projects.
exam_trap: Treating laws/regulations as OPAs.

title: Knowledge areas (overview)
definition: Knowledge areas group related PM work (scope, schedule, cost, quality, resources, communications, risk, procurement, stakeholders).
why_it_matters: PMP checks your conceptual map for where project work belongs and how areas interact.
example: A scope change impacts schedule and cost; integration coordinates the decision.
exam_trap: Treating knowledge areas as sequential phases.

title: Integration management (early focus)
definition: Integration management coordinates project work; early integration includes developing the charter and aligning objectives with strategy.
key_points: Develop Project Charter is an integration process
why_it_matters: Initiation questions often tie charter work to integration management.
example: PM aligns charter objectives with strategy and constraints before baselines exist.
exam_trap: Assuming integration is only change control.

title: Project boundaries
definition: Project boundaries define high-level in-scope and out-of-scope areas during initiation, setting expectations before detailed scope definition.
key_points: Clarifies what the project will not do
why_it_matters: PMP tests preventing scope conflict by setting boundaries early.
example: Charter covers appointment scheduling but excludes billing redesign.
exam_trap: Vague boundaries like 'modernize systems' with no exclusions.

title: High-level risks (initiation)
definition: High-level risks are early uncertainties identified during initiation to inform feasibility and charter decisions.
key_points: Not a full risk register yet
why_it_matters: PMP expects early risk awareness, even at a high level.
example: Risk: contracting may take 10 weeks, threatening a fixed deadline constraint.
exam_trap: Waiting until execution to identify risks.

title: Requirements sources
definition: Requirements come from business documents, stakeholder needs, regulations, and operational constraints; initiation clarifies sources and ownership.
why_it_matters: PMP tests identifying the right sources and stakeholders before documenting requirements.
example: Compliance defines retention; operations defines workflow; users define usability needs.
exam_trap: Only gathering requirements from the loudest stakeholders.

title: Constraints and trade-offs
definition: Schedule, cost, and scope constraints are interdependent; changing one usually affects others and may impact quality and risk.
why_it_matters: Questions often ask what to do when deadlines/budgets change—negotiate trade-offs.
example: To shorten schedule, add cost (overtime) or reduce scope.
exam_trap: Accepting impossible constraints without negotiating options.

title: PM appointment timing
definition: Best practice is to assign the PM as early as possible during initiation to influence charter and early stakeholder/requirement decisions.
why_it_matters: PMP often asks when the PM should be assigned—early is best practice.
example: PM attends charter workshops to validate milestones and constraints.
exam_trap: Assigning PM after key commitments are already made.

title: Responsibility vs authority
definition: Responsibility is accountability for outcomes; authority is the power to make decisions—misalignment creates friction.
why_it_matters: Many scenarios describe accountability without authority; correct action is to clarify and escalate appropriately.
example: PM accountable for delivery but can’t get SME time; escalates priority conflicts to sponsor.
exam_trap: Trying to solve authority gaps by working harder instead of addressing governance.

title: Mandated technology/vendor
definition: If governance mandates a tool or vendor, it is a constraint that must be documented and planned around.
why_it_matters: PMP tests documenting constraints and managing expectations rather than changing mandates informally.
example: Company mandates a specific cloud; PM aligns solution, budget, and schedule to that constraint.
exam_trap: Treating mandates as optional or changing them without approval.

title: Stakeholder engagement strategy (initiation)
definition: Early engagement focuses on identifying key stakeholders, understanding expectations, and setting communication/participation appropriate to influence.
why_it_matters: Many failures start with stakeholder resistance; PMP tests proactive engagement.
example: Meet department heads early to surface impacts and concerns before commitments are made.
exam_trap: Delaying stakeholder conversations until planning is complete.

title: Selection vs authorization
definition: Selection is typically a portfolio/governance decision; authorization happens via charter, after which the PM leads planning and execution.
key_points: PM may provide input but usually doesn’t decide
why_it_matters: Clarifies who starts projects and what the PM can do at each stage.
example: Portfolio approves; sponsor issues charter; PM begins detailed planning.
exam_trap: Assuming the PM can start/cancel projects unilaterally.

title: Measurable objectives and success criteria
definition: Charter objectives should be measurable; success criteria define how outcomes and benefits alignment will be judged.
key_points: Clarify success at a high level
why_it_matters: PMP tests preventing scope confusion by defining success early.
example: Objective: reduce cycle time by 20% within 6 months after go-live.
exam_trap: Vague objectives like 'improve efficiency' with no metrics.

title: Summary milestone schedule
definition: The charter may include a summary milestone schedule without detailed sequencing.
why_it_matters: Exam questions ask what schedule detail belongs in the charter.
example: Milestones: charter approved, requirements complete, pilot launch, full rollout.
exam_trap: Including a full network diagram/critical path in the charter.

title: Budget summary vs cost baseline
definition: The charter often sets a budget cap or funding range; the detailed cost baseline is created later during planning.
why_it_matters: PMP tests the difference between early authorization numbers and baselined plans.
example: Charter sets cap at $500k; planning produces estimates, reserves, and baseline.
exam_trap: Treating early rough numbers as final cost baseline.

title: Requirements vs solution design
definition: Requirements describe what is needed; solution design decides how to meet them and should not be locked in prematurely.
why_it_matters: PMP scenarios often include stakeholders prescribing a solution; clarify needs first.
example: Stakeholder asks for 'a new app'; PM probes the requirement: 'mobile access to scheduling.'
exam_trap: Locking in a solution before understanding requirements/constraints.

title: Initiation deliverables vs planning deliverables
definition: Initiation deliverables authorize and frame the project; planning deliverables create baselines and detailed plans for execution and control.
key_points: Planning: PM plan, baselines, detailed requirements
why_it_matters: This distinction appears in many PMP questions about what happens next.
example: After charter approval, the PM develops the project management plan and scope baseline.
exam_trap: Skipping initiation artifacts and jumping to baselines without authorization.
